question_text: In the Northeast of China, which climatic variables increase from northwest to southeast during each growing stage?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Relative humidity, cloud cover, and atmospheric pressure'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Crop evapotranspiration (ETc), drought frequency, and drought risk'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Precipitation, CWSDI, wet frequency, and wet risk'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Temperature, solar radiation, and wind speed'}]
source_facts: [1] The precipitation, CWSDI, wet frequency, and wet risk increased from northwest to southeast for each growing stage, while crop evapotranspiration (ETc), drought frequency and drought risk showed the opposite characteristics.

question_text: A vineyard manager notices that some vines are showing stunted shoot growth and failure to bud out on some canes or spurs, a year after infection with Pierce's disease. The manager is considering severe pruning and training up a new trunk a few inches above the graft union. Given the research on recovery from Pierce's disease, what is the likelihood that this management practice will be successful?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': "Highly likely, as most vines recover from Pierce's disease after severe pruning"}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Cannot be determined without more information on the specific vineyard conditions'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Unlikely, as symptoms are likely to reappear the second year even with severe pruning'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Depends on the date of infection and winter temperatures following infection'}]
source_facts:
[1] Research has shown that severe pruning – cutting a few inches above the graft union – and training up a new trunk is not a viable management practice; Pierce's disease symptoms reappear the second year.
---
[2] A year after the vines are infected some canes or spurs may fail to bud out, and shoot growth is stunted.
---
[3] Some vines recover from Pierce's disease; the probability of recovery depends on the date of infection and temperatures in the winter following infection.

question_text: A winery in the Northeast is seeing serious downy mildew pressure in a wet season. The vineyard manager warns that if the outbreak is not checked, vines could lose enough foliage to halt this year’s fruit maturation and also compromise cane maturation for the following crop. Spray records also show repeated use of products from FRAC groups 11, 40, and P07, and the team has learned that resistance to those groups is often found in vineyards across this region. As harvest approaches, which disease-control adjustment is the most defensible choice?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Increase the dose of FRAC 11 products only, because resistance concerns apply mainly earlier in the year'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Stop treating and accept leaf loss, because fruit maturity is already set by late season'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Shift late-season control toward contact-type protectant materials, since they carry lower resistance risk and can still work at that stage'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Rely mainly on FRAC 11, 40, and P07 again late in the season because they are already familiar to the crew'}]
source_facts:
[1] If you let downy mildew get out of control, it can strip vines of their leaves and in the worst cases, effectively end fruit ripening for the year, and shoot ripening for next year’s crop.
---
[2] Conversely, the surface protectants would be the least risky in terms of the development of resistance and can be an effective means of controlling downy mildew late into the growing season.
---
[3] Recent research by the University of Maryland, Virginia Tech, and Penn State, has determined that downy mildew resistance to FRAC 11, 40, and P07 may be common in wine grape vineyards of the Northeastern and Mid-Atlantic grape growing regions.
